Facts.text: Zeno is crowned co-emperor of the Byzantine Empire with his son Leo II (age 6 or 7)
Rashidun Caliphate, then the largest empire in history, ends with the death of its leader, Ali. Succeeded by the Umayyad Caliphate.
Sergius III comes out of retirement to take over the papacy from the deposed antipope Christopher
Mongols defeated by Dai Viet at the battle of Dong Bo Dau
Sermon of Constance: Zwingli defends 67 theses
Battle of the Scheldt: Dutch rebel Sea Beggar fleet led by Lodewijk van Boisot defeats the Spanish and sinks 15 Spanish ships
Deventer and Zutphen surrender to Spain
Feodor III succeeds his father and becomes Tsar of Russia
John Gays' satiric work "The Beggar's Opera" premieres at the Lincoln's Inn Fields Theatre in London, England [NS=Feb 9]
Paris churchyard of Saint-Medard closed after Jansenistic ritual
John Beckley of Virginia appointed first Librarian of Congress
War of the Sixth Coalition: Russia and Prussia are defeated by France in the Battle of Brienne
President Jackson orders first use of US troops to suppress a labor dispute
Sicily accepts new Constitution (choose parliament/freedom of press)
Victoria Cross is established to acknowledge valor in the face of the enemy (United Kingdom and Commonwealth countries)
American College established in Rome by Pope Pius IX
US state of Kansas admitted to the Union as the 34th state
Bear River Massacre: American soldiers slaughter hundreds of Native Americans at the confluence of the Bear River and Beaver Creek in present day Idaho
Francis L Cardoza elected State Treasurer of South Carolina
Liliuokalani is proclaimed Queen of Hawaii, its last monarch
The Coca-Cola Company is incorporated in Atlanta, Georgia
King Koko's Kopermannen assault on Akassa Niger, 100's killed
American Emile Grubbe is the first doctor to use radiation treatment for breast cancer
American League organized in Buffalo, Chicago, Cleveland, Detroit, Indianapolis, Kansas City, Milwaukee and Minneapolis
Dutch railroad workers strike
1st athletic letters given (University of Chicago football team)
Dutch speed skater Coen de King skates a world record 32,370m in one hour
Alpha Phi Alpha Fraternity, at Cornell University, incorporates
Martial law declared in textile strike in Lawrence, Massachusetts
Alpha Kappa Alpha Sorority, at Howard University, incorporates
1st bombing of Paris by German Zeppelins takes place
British submarine K13 sank in Gaire Loch, Scotland; killing 32 of her crew
Ukrainian-Soviet War: Battle of Kruty
Great Olympic Blowdown: Hurricane force winds hit US Pacific Northwest states of Washington and Oregon
Union of Costa Rica, Guatemala, Honduras and El Salvador dissolved
1st flight of autogiro (Juan de la Cierva, Madrid Spain)
Ice cream cone rolling machine patented by Carl Taylor, Cleveland
4th German government of Marx forms
Seeing Eye Guide Dog organization forms in USA
Alexandros Koryzis becomes Prime Minister of Greece
First broadcast of Roy Plomley's "Desert Island Discs" on BBC Radio
German & Italian troops occupy Benghazi, Libya
Peru and Ecuador sign Protocol of Rio (boundary determination)
New Zealand cruiser Kiwi collides with Japanese submarine I-1 at Guadalcanal
Sidney Kingsley's play "Patriots" premieres in NYC
U.S. cruiser "Chicago" is heavily damaged by Japanese bombers on the first day of the Battle of Rennell Island
USS Missouri, the last battleship commissioned by the US Navy, is launched
World War II: 285 German bombers attack London
World War II: About 38 men, women, and children die in the Koniuchy massacre in Poland
World War II: The Battle of Cisterna takes place in central Italy
Commissioner Happy Chandler fines the Yankees, Cubs and Phillies $500 each for signing high school players
Pakistan Socialist Party is founded
Great Britain, Belgium, Luxembourg, Netherlands, New Zealand and Switzerland recognize Israel
Baseball signs 6 year All-Star pact for TV-radio rights for $6 million
Arnold Schoenberg's last musical work "De Profundis" premieres in Cologne, Germany
John Williams Cox buys Yankee Stadium, sells grounds to Knights of Columbus, later leaves structure to Rice University (1962)
Murderer, Charles Starkweather, captured by police in Wyoming
Smog pollution with readings higher than the 1952 'Great Smog of London' hits London, with many suffering chest and lung-related illnesses
Walt Disney's "Sleeping Beauty" released
Baseball's new Continental League awards its 8th, and last, franchise to Buffalo, New York
IX Winter Olympic Games open in Innsbruck, Austria
Most lopsided high-school basketball score 211-29 (Louisiana)
NBC purchases AFL 5 year (1965-69) TV rights for $36 million

The Beatles record German language versions of their hits "Komm, Gib Mir Deine Hand" ("I Want to Hold Your Hand") and "Sie Liebt Dich" ("She Loves You") at EMI's Pathé Marconi Studios in Paris, France
Unmanned Apollo 1 Saturn launcher test attains Earth orbit
Snow storm in north east US kills 165
Kees Verkerk becomes European skating champ
The Arusha Declaration written by Tanzanian President Julius Nyerere is issued, clarifying Tanzania's path towards Ujamaa (African socialism)
WDAZ TV channel 8 in Devils Lake, ND (NBC) begins broadcasting
Nauru (formerly Pleasant Island) adopts constitution
Sheahan & Connolly hang on for exciting draw Australia v West Indies
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Test debut of Dennis Keith Lillee, v England at Adelaide
International agreement to pay for and conserve 9th century Borobudur Temple in Indonesia, world's largest Buddhist temple (completed 1982). Beginnings of the World Heritage Convention.
1974 NFL Draft: Ed Jones from Tennessee State first pick by Dallas Cowboys
Kuwait announces 60 percent government participation in BP-Gulf concession
First American Annual Comedy Awards, hosted by Alan King
West Indies win fifth test against India to win an exciting series 3-2
Bombs set off in London's West End by the IRA; 1 person is injured
Zeiss planetarium in the Hague destroyed by fire

Brenda Spencer (16) kills 2 adults and injures 8 children and a police officer in a San Diego, California schoolyard shooting; incident inspires Irish rockers Boomtown Rats' song "I Don't Like Mondays"
English progressive rock trio Emerson, Lake & Palmer disband after 10 years together
6 Iranian-held US hostages escape with help of Canadians
Cleveland Cavaliers beat LA Lakers 154-153 in quadruple OT
AL approves sale of White Sox to Jerry Reinsdorf & Eddie Einhorn for $20 million, & 80% of Mariners to George Argyros for $104 million
Old Dominion ends Louisiana Tech's women's basketball record 54-game winning streak
Wayne Garland, baseball's first millionaire free agent, is waived by the Indians
"Down Under" by Men At Work hits #1 on UK pop chart
Yoweri Museveni sworn in as President of Uganda
The president of the Philippines puts down a rebellion against their government in Manila
Canadian Ben Johnson breaks own 50-yard dash world record at 5.15
Detroit Pistons draw a then record NBA regular season crowd of 61,983 fans to the Pontiac Silverdome for a 125-108 win over the Boston Celtics
Detroit Tigers star outfielder Kirk Gibson signs a 3-year contract with LA Dodgers
United Airlines Boeing 747SP, circles world in 36h54m15s
Cleveland's Chris Dudley misses 5 free throws during 1 foul attempt
Dow jumps 38.06 recoups 508-pt loss since Oct 1987; index at 2,256.43
Episcopal Church of the United States, Diocese of Massachusetts elects Barbara Harris as its first female bishop
Long Island preacher Gene Profeta pleads guilty to tax evasion
Orlando Arena opens in Orlando, Florida
USSR's Phobos II enters Martian orbit
Exxon Valdez capt Joseph Hazelwood goes on trial due to oil spill
The Battle of Khafji begins, the first major ground engagement of the Gulf War

Frankie Randall beats Mexican boxing legend Julio César Chávez by split decision to win WBC super lightweight title at MGM Grand, Las Vegas; Chavez's 1st knockdown of career in 11th and 1st loss in 91 pro fights
Greg Blewett scores century on Test debut vs England, Adelaide
La Fenice, Venice's opera house, is destroyed by fire
The 6,138th performance of "Cats" is held in London, surpassing the record of Broadway's longest-running musical, "A Chorus Line"
Singer Bobby Brown found guilty of DWI in Fort Lauderdale, Florida
Soyuz TM-27 launches to MIR
Thick Fog causes highway carnage in Belgium and Netherlands, 6 die
Woman's Clinic in Birmingham, Alabama bombed, 1 killed
Thousands of student protesters in Indonesia storm parliament and demand that President Abdurrahman Wahid resign due to alleged involvement in corruption scandals
"Foolish" single released by Ashanti (Billboard Song of the Year 2002)
Sally Clark cleared of murdering her 2 sons after more than 3 years of prison in what was a life sentence
The first direct commercial flights from the mainland China (from Guangzhou) to Taiwan since 1949 arrives in Taipei. Shortly afterwards, a China Airlines carrier lands in Beijing.
Irfan Pathan becomes first bowler to take a Test cricket hat-trick in the opening over of a match
Rod Blagojevich, Governor of Illinois, is removed from office after being convicted of corruption charges
Archaeologists discover the oldest Roman Temple (6th C BC) at Sant’Omobono
Bohuslav Sobotka becomes Prime Minister of the Czech Republic
Scientists discover how to convert normal cells into stem cells in mice
Attack on mosque in Quebec kills 6 and injures 17, shooter is French Canadian student
Cleveland Indians announce they will remove "Chief Wahoo" caricature logo from uniforms in 2019
F.B.I deputy director Andrew G. McCabe steps down from office
Toronto police arrest landscaper Bruce McArthur for murder after remains of at least 5 people found in potted plants
"Empire" star Jussie Smollett suffers suspected racist and homophobic attack in Chicago; later determined to be a self-perpetrated hoax
Approaching polar vortex prompts state of emergency to be declared in Wisconsin, Michigan, Illinois, Alabama and Mississippi. US Postal Service suspends deliveries to ten states.
Norwegian government proposes building a floating road tunnel as part of a new roadway between Kristiansand and Trondheim
Tehran, Iran bans dog walking in public, and driving with dogs
Toronto landscaper Bruce McArthur admits in court to the murder of eight gay men
Number of COVID-19 cases passes those of SARS with over 7,700 cases in China confirmed, with 170 deaths
"Proposal to Prevent the Feminisation of Male Adolescents" published by China's Education Ministry asks schools to use sports to cultivate masculinity
Johnson & Johnson's Janssen single-dose vaccine shown to be 66% effective in trials, with complete protection against hospitalization and death
Nigerian farmers win landmark case against Shell at Court of Appeal of the Hague - held accountable for oil spills and ordered to carry out a clean-up in Delta region
Novavax vaccine shows 89% efficacy in UK trials, including against UK variant
"Suits" was the most-streamed show in 2023 with 57.7 billion minutes, followed by "Bluey" then "NCIS" according to Nielsen
Chinese property giant Evergrande with more than $300 billion in debt is ordered to liquidate by a Hong Kong court
Elmo the Muppet posts  "Elmo is just checking in! How is everybody doing?" on X - receives thousands of brutally honest replies

US Army helicopter practicing night vision flying, crashes mid-air into commercial jet, killing all 67 passengers, including dozens of figure skaters, and crew members, over the Potomac River while  approaching Ronald Reagan National Airport near Washington D.C.
MathematicianJohn Napierdedicates his "Plaine Discovery of the Whole Revelation of St. John" to KingJames VI, predicts end of the world in 1688 or 1700
William Shakespeare's play "Romeo and Juliet" is thought to have been first performed. Officially published early 1597.
"La Fiera di Venezia (The Fair of Venice)", a commedia per musica  byAntonio Salierifirst performed at Carnival in Venice
WolfgangMozart's dramatic opera "Idomeneo" premieres at the Cuvilliés Theatre in Munich, Germany
In a surprising announcement,John Hancockresigns as Governor of Massachusetts, allegedly due to his failing health
First celebration of Burns Night, in honor of poetRobert Burns's birthday by The Mother Club in Greenock (later realized his actual birthday 25th January)
American writerEdgar Allan Poe's poem "The Raven" 1st published in The Evening Mirror newspaper (New York City)
Custer BattlefieldNational Monument established in Montana
Karl Benzpatents the "Benz Patent-Motorwagen" in Karlsruhe, Germany, the world's first automobile with an internal combustion engine

TsarNicolas IIof Russia, unsettled by the rising violence and protest, enacts reforms to improve the conditions of workers; these changes will do little to stop disorder throughout Russia in ensuing months
RepublicanCharles Curtisof Kansas becomes the 1st Native American US Senator
Walt Disneystarts work as an artist with KC Slide Co for $40 a week
British Liberal Party choosesDavid Lloyd Georgeas leader
First players elected to the Baseball Hall of Fame:Ty Cobb,Babe Ruth,Honus Wagner,Christy MathewsonandWalter Johnson
Arthur Miller's play "All My Sons" premieres in NYC
Friedrich Durrenmatt's "Der Besuch der Alten Dame" premieres in Zurich
Graham Greene's stage drama "The Potting Shed" opens at the  Bijou Theatre, later transferring to the John Golden Theatre, NYC; runs for 143 performances
Jim Thorpe,Red Grange&George Halaselected to football Hall of Fame
"Dr Strangelove", directed byStanley Kubrickand starringPeter SellersandGeorge C. Scott, premieres
Branch Rickeyand Lloyd Waner elected to Baseball Hall of Fame
Chinese Vice-PremierDeng Xiaopingvisits Washington, D.C., the highest ranking Chinese Communist Party official to do so
US PresidentJimmy CartercommutesPatty Hearst's 7 year jail sentence to time served (22 months)
President Reaganformally announces he will seek a 2nd term
Jari Kurriof Edmonton Oilers scores 100th pt of season in game 39
Oxford University refuses to award Prime MinisterMargaret Thatcheran honorary degree
William J. Caseyterm as 13th director of the CIA ends
Game-winning RBI, official statistic dropped after 9 years of use NY MetsKeith Hernandezis the all-time leader with 129
Nelson Mandelaand anti-Apartheid leader and Zulu prince Mangosuthu Buthelezi meet after 28 years
US postal service issues a stamp commemorating chemistPercy Lavon Julian
Super Bowl XXIX, Joe Robbie Stadium, Miami, FL: San Francisco 49ers beat San Diego Chargers, 49-26; MVP:Steve Young, SF, QB
France will no longer test nuclear weapons, its presidentJacques Chiracsays, following international outcry over tests in the Pacific

Karl Maloneof the Utah Jazz becomes the 3rd player in NBA history to score 30,000 career points; finishes with 35 in a 96-94 loss to Minnesota; behindKareem Abdul-Jabbar(38,387) and Wilt Chamberlain (31,419)
The San Diego Chargers hireMarty Schottenheimeras their 13th head coach
US PresidentGeorge W. Bushin his State of the Union address describes "regimes that sponsor terror" as an "Axis of Evil," which includes Iraq, Iran, and North Korea
US Postal Service releases 39-cent stamp featuringHattie McDanielin the dress she wore in 1940 when she became the 1st African-American actress to accept an Academy Award
Ken Dryden's #29 jersey is retired by the Montreal Canadiens
US PresidentBarack Obamasigns his first bill into law: The Lilly Ledbetter Fair Pay Act of 2009
American snowboarderShaun Whiteachieves the first ever SuperPipe perfect score (100) in Winter X Games history
Cristiano Ronaldobecomes the 1st non-Spanish player to captain Real Madrid, making his 500th appearance for the club
Dale Jarrettis inducted into the NASCAR Hall of Fame
David Beckhamlaunches a Major League Soccer team in Florida; Inter Miami CF
Marvelfilm "Black Panther" directed by Ryan Coogler and starringChadwick Bosemanpremieres in Los Angeles
In the aftermath to a wild ending to UFC 229, Khabib Nurmagomedov ($500k & 9 months) andConor McGregor($50k & 6 months) are issued fines and suspensions; bans retroactive to October 6, 2018
Elon Musktweets his company Neuralink has implanted a ‘brain-reading’ device into a person for the first time
First Governor of New Zealand and co-author of the Treaty of Waitangi Captain William Hobson arrives in the Bay of Islands, NZ
SenatorHenry Claydrafts the Compromise of 1850 to defuse tensions between slave states and free states over territories won during the Mexican–American War
Swiss team of Denis Vaucher (C), Alfred Aufdenblatten, Antoine Julen & Alfons Julen win the military patrol gold medal at the Chamonix Winter Olympics; first version of the biathlon
Malaysia officially declares the disappearance of missing flight MH370 an accident
Johann Georg Graevius, German classical scholar and critic, born in Naumburg, Electorate of Saxony, Germany (d. 1703)
Hubert K. Poot, Dutch poet (Akkerleven (Field Life); Minnezangen (Love Songs)), born in Abtswoude, Dutch Republic (d. 1733)
Carlmann Kolb, German composer and priest, born in Kösslarn, Lower Bavaria (d. 1765)
Giuseppe Bonno, Austrian composer, born in Vienna (d. 1788)
Georg Christoph Wagenseil, Austrian court composer and pianist, born in Vienna (d. 1777)
Jeffrey Amherst, English army general (commanded British forces in North America) and Royal Governor of Virginia (1759-1768), born in Sevenoaks, England (d. 1797)
Paul Rabaut, French pastor and leader of the Huguenots, born in Bédarieux, France (d. 1794)[1][2]
Christian VII, King of Denmark and Norway (1766-1808), born in Christiansborg Palace, Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1808)
Moses Cleaveland, American politician and founder of Cleveland, Ohio, born in Canterbury, Connecticut (d. 1806)
Henry Lee III "Light Horse Harry", American Congressman, Governor of Virginia and American General, born on Leesylvania Plantation, Colony of Virginia (d. 1818)
Albert Gallatin, Swiss-American politician, Secretary of the Treasury (1801-14), born in Geneva, Switzerland (d. 1849)
Johann Gottfried Seume, German writer (Spaziergang nach Syrakus), born in Poserna, Saxony-Anhalt, (d. 1810)
Friedrich Mohs, German mineralogist and creator of the Mohs scale of mineral hardness, born in Gernrode, Germany (d. 1839)
Daniel François Esprit Auber, French opera composer (Fra Diavolo), born in Caen, Normandy, France (d. 1871)
Franciszek Ścigalski, Polish violinist, composer, conductor, and teacher, born in Grodzisk Wielkopolski, Poland (d. 1846)
Vasily Zhukovsky, Russian folk poet and translator (Homerus) [NS=Feb 9], born in Mishenskoe, Tula Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1852)
Henry Neele, English poet, born in London, England (d. 1828)
Johannes Bernardus van Bree, Dutch violinist, composer, and conductor (Felix Meritis Society, 1829-57), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1857)
James Outram “the Bayard of India”, English general and administrator in India, born in Bulterley Hall, Derbyshire (d. 1868)
Ernst Kummer, German mathematician (Kummer surface), born in Sorau, Prussia (d. 1893)[1]
Isaac Ferdinand Quinby, American General (Union Army), born in Morristown, New Jersey (d. 1891)
Franklin Gardner, American Major General (Confederate Army), born in New York City (d. 1873)
Karl von Perfall, German composer and music director (Royal Bavarian Court and Residence Theater, 1868-92), born in Munich, Germany (d. 1907)
Benjamin Franklin Potts, American lawyer, politician, and Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Fox Township, Ohio (d. 1887)
James Meech Warner, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Middlebury, Vermont (d. 1897)
Karol Olszewski, Polish scientist, mathematician, and first person along with Zygmunt Wróblewski to liquify oxygen and nitrogen, born in Broniszów, Galicia (d. 1915)
Ebenezer Howard, English pioneer of garden cities, born in London, England (d. 1928)
Lawrence Hargrave, English-born aviation pioneer and inventor (box kite), born in London (d. 1915)
Frederic Hymen Cowen, British pianist, conductor, and composer (Thorgrim), born in Kingston, Jamaica (d. 1935)
Ion Luca Caragiale, Romanian actor and playwright (O Noapte Furtunoasa), born in Haimanalele, Ploiesti, Romania (d. 1912)
Frederick Delius, British composer (A Mass of Life; Brigg Fair; In a Summer Garden), born in Bradford, England (d. 1934)
Adolf Philipp, German-American Broadway composer (Adele), born in Hamburg, German Confederation (d. 1936)
Romain Rolland, French writer (Jean-Christophe, Nobel Prize for Literature 1915), born in Clamecy, France (d. 1944)
Vicente Blasco Ibáñez, Spanish writer (4 Horsemen of Apocalypse), born in Valencia, Spain (d. 1928)
Andrey Scherbachov, Russian composer and pianist, born in St Petersburg, Russia (d. 1922)
Eduardo Lopez-Chavarri y Marco, Spanish composer, born in Valencia, Spain (d. 1970)
Prince Luigi Amedeo, Duke of the Abruzzi, Italian navy officer, explorer and mountaineer (Ruwenzori Range, Uganda; Mount Saint Alias, Yukon), born in Madrid, Spain (d. 1933)
Robert Lach, Austrian composer, born in Vienna (d. 1958)
Ludolf Nielsen, Danish composer, born in Nørre Tvede, Denmark (d. 1939)
[William] Havergal Brian, British composer ("Gothic" symphony), born in Dresden, Staffordshire (d. 1972)
Georges Catroux, French Army general, French Ambassador to the Soviet Union (1945-48) and Grand Chancellor of the Légion d'honneur (1954-69), born in Limoges, France (d. 1969)[1]
Barney Oldfield, American auto racer (International Motorsports Hall of Fame; first to drive at 60mph on circular track), born near Wauseon, Ohio (d. 1946)
W. C. Fields [William Claude Dukenfield], American actor (Bank Dick), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1946)
Juhan Aavic, Estonian composer, born in Holstre, Russian Empire (d. 1982)
Albert Conti, Austrian actor (Jazz Heaven, Doomed Battalion), born in Trieste, Austria-Hungary (d. 1967)
Francisco Santiago, Filipino pianist, composer (Taga-ilog Symphony), conductor, and pedagogue, born in Santa Maria, Bulacan, Philippines (d. 1947)
Rudolf Mauersberger, German choral conductor and composer (Dresdner Requiem), born in Mauersberg, Kingdom of Saxony, German Empire (d. 1971)
Marguerite Canal, French conductor and composer (1st woman in France to conduct an orchestra), born in Toulouse, France (d. 1978)
Clifford Gray, American bobsledder (Olympic gold 5-man 1928, 4-man 1932), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1969)
Ernst Lubitsch, German actor, producer and film director (To Be or Not to Be, Love Parade), born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1947)
Edric Cundell, British composer (Serbia; The Tragedy of Deirdre) conductor, and educator (Guildhall School of Music, 1938-56), born in London, England (d. 1961)
Martian Negrea, Romanian composer and conductor, born in Valea Viilor, Romania (d. 1973)
Muna Lee, American poet and activist, born in Raymond, Mississippi (d. 1965)
Teddy Hoad, West Indies cricket batsman, captain (4 Tests; Barbados), born in Richmond, Saint Michael, Barbados (d. 1986)
Fernand Quinet, Belgian cellist, composer and conductor (La Guerre), born in Charleroi, Belgium (d. 1971)
Karl Bjarnhof, Danish blind journalist and writer (The Stars Grow Pale), born in Vejle, Denmark (d. 1980)
Marco Tajcevic, Serbian composer, born in Osijek, Austria-Hungry (d. 1984)
Willem Hussem, Dutch painter and poet (Coastline, Lookout on Sea), born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1974)
Allen B. DuMont, American engineer and inventor (perfected commercial practical cathode ray tube), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1965)
Cornelis Hendrik Edelman, Dutch geologist, born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1964)
Barnett Newmann, American abstract expressionist painter (Black Fire I), born in New York City (d. 1970)
Frans Hin, Dutch yachtsman (Olympic gold 12' Dinghy 1920; aged 14 years 163 days), born in Haarlem, Netherlands (d. 1968)
Joe Primeau, Canadian Hockey HOF center (Stanley Cup, Lady Byng Memorial Trophy 1931–32; Toronto Maple Leafs) and coach (Stanley Cup 1950-51 Toronto Maple Leafs), born in Lindsay, Ontario (d. 1989)
Alan Marshal, Australian actor (White Cliffs of Dover, Lydia), born in Sydney, New South Wales (d. 1961)
George Thomas, 1st Viscount Tonypandy, British politician (Speaker of the House of Commons), born in Port Talbot, Wales (d. 1997)
Colin Middleton, Northern Irish landscape artist and surrealist, born in Belfast, Northern Ireland (d. 1983)
Paul Hodder-Williams, British publisher (Hodder & Stoughton), born in Calcutta, India (d. 2007)
Peter von Siemens, German industrialist, born in Charlottenburg, German Empire (d. 1986)
Peter von Zahn, German journalist, writer, and film maker, born in Chemnitz, Germany (d. 2001)
Victor Mature, American actor (I Wake Up Screaming, Samson and Delilah), born in Louisville, Kentucky (d. 1999)
Menelaos Pallantios, Greek composer, born in Piraeus (d. 2012)
Fred Ramsey, American jazz writer, born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1995)
Halfdan Rasmussen, Danish poet (wrote about social issues and nonsense verse for children), born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 2002)
John Serry, Sr., American classical, jazz, and bolero accordionist, arranger, composer, organist, and educator, born in Brooklyn, NYC (d. 2003)
Barbara Skelton, English writer, memoirist, and socialite (mistress to King Farouk), born in Taplow, England (d. 1996)
Kyra Vayne [Knopmuss], English stage and opera soprano, born in St. Petersburg, Russia (d. 2001)
John Raitt, American stage and screen singer and actor (Carousel; The Pajama Game), born in Santa Ana, California (d. 2005)
Bill Rigney, American baseball infielder (MLB All Star 1948; NY Giants) and manager (NY/SF Giants; LA Angels; Minnesota Twins), born in Alameda, California (d. 2001)
Doyle Royal, American collegiate soccer coach (NCAA C'ship 1968; University of Maryland 1946-73) and tennis coach (U of Maryland 1954-80), born in Washington, D.C. (d. 2020)
Norman Frederick Simpson, British playwright (One Way Pendulum), born in London, England (d. 2011)
Alec Coppen, British pioneering psychiatrist (serotonin theory of depression), born in London, England (d. 2019)
José Luis de Villalonga, 9th Marquess of Castellbell, Spanish aristocrat, author, and film actor, born in Madrid, Spain (d. 2007)
Paul Gayten American R&B pianist, songwriter, bandleader, producer (Clarence "Frogman" Henry, Chuck Berry), and record company executive (Chess Records), born in Kentwood, Louisiana (d. 1991)
Anthony George, American actor (Checkmate; Dark Shadows), born in Endicott, New York (d. 2005)
Gerda Steinhoff, Nazi concentration camp overseer, born in Danzig-Langfuhr, Free City of Danzig (d. 1946)
(Sidney) "Paddy" Chayefsky, American Academy Award-winning screenwriter (Marty; The Hospital; Network), novelist, and playwright, born in the Bronx, New York City (d. 1981)
Eddie Taylor, American electric blues guitarist and singer, born in Benoit, Mississippi (d. 1985)
Jack Burke Jr, American golfer (US Masters, PGA C'ship 1956; Ryder Cup 1951, 53, 55, 59; 1973 [non-playing captain]), born in Fort Worth, Texas (d. 2024)
Brian Trubshaw, British test pilot, first to fly Concorde, born in Liverpool (d. 2001)
Lord John Gregson, British Labour politician and Baron, born in Manchester, England (d. 2009)
Luigi Nono, Italian avant-garde composer (Canonic Variations), born in Venice, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1990)
Marcelle Ferron, Canadian painter, stained glass artist, and major figure in the Quebec contemporary art scene, born in Louiseville, Quebec (d. 2001)
Peter Voulkos, American abstract ceramic artist, born in Bozeman, Montana (d. 2002)
Abdus Salam, Pakistani theoretical physicist (1979 Noble Prize for Physics - electroweak unification theory), born in Jhand, British India (d. 1996)
Bob Falkenburg, American tennis player (Wimbledon singles 1948, doubles, 1947; US Open doubles 1944), born in New York City (d. 2022)
Edward Abbey, American author and environmentalist (Desert Solitaire), born in Indiana, Pennsylvania (d. 1989)
Bengt Hambraeus, Swedish Canadian composer and organist, born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 2000)
Elio Petri, Italian director (Property is no theft), born in Rome, Italy (d. 1982)
Puggy Pearson, American poker player (World Series of Poker Main Event 1973; 4 x World Series of Poker bracelets), born in Las Vegas, Nevada (d. 2006)
William McMillan, American sports shooter (Olympic gold 25m rapid fire pistol 1960; World C'ship gold 1952), born in Frostburg, Maryland (d. 2000)
Derek Bailey, British avant-garde jazz guitar virtuoso, born in Sheffield, England (d. 2005)
John Junkin, British actor and scriptwriter (A Hard Day's Night), born in Ealing, Middlesex (d. 2006)

Leslie Bricusse, British stage and film composer and lyricist (Doctor Dolittle; Goodbye, Mr. Chips; "Goldfinger"; Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory - "The Candy Man"; "Pure Imagination"), born in Pinner, Middlesex, England (d. 2021)
Beverly Kenney American jazz singer ("I Hate Rock n' Roll"; "Born To Be Blue"), born in Harrison, New Jersey (d. 1960)
Raman Subba Row, English cricket batsman (13 Tests, 3 x 100, HS 137; Surrey CCC, Northamptonshire CCC), born in London (d. 2024)
Tommy Taylor, English soccer striker (19 caps; Manchester United 166 games; died Munich air disaster), born in Smithies, West Riding of Yorkshire (d. 1958)
R. C. Alston, British bibliographer, born in Trinidad (d. 2011)
Ron Townson, American rock vocalist (The 5th Dimension - "Up, Up And Away"; "Aquarius/Let the Sunshine In"), born in St Louis, Missouri (d. 2001)
Sacha Distel, French singer ("Raindrops Keep Falling on My Head" cover), born in Paris, France (d. 2004)
Wolfgang Gönnenwein, German choral director and educator, and artistic director (Ludwigsburger Schlossfestspiele, 1972-2004), born in Schwäbisch Hall, Germany (d. 2015)
Noel Harrison, British singer ("The Windmills of Your Mind"), and actor (Girl from U.N.C.L.E. - "Mark Slate"), born in London, England (d. 2013)
Paul Gutama Soegijo, Indonesian composer working ijn Germany, born in Yogyakarta, Dutch East Indies (d. 2019)
Roger Payne American biologist, environmentalist, and whale conservationist (Songs of the Humpback Whale), born in Manhattan, New York (d. 2023)[1]
James Jamerson, American session pop and R&B bass guitarist (Motown Records), born in Edisto, South Carolina (d. 1983)
Malcolm Binns, English concert pianist, born in Nottingham, England
